@graph.name: 9 Materials That Help With Eye Pressing
@graph.description: Your child presses on their eyes because something about that pressure or those patterns meets a deep sensory need. You can't just stop it — you have to replace it. Here are nine materials that meet the need without risking their vision.

mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter presses on her eyes constantly, and i'm running out of ways to keep her safe. this isn't the occasional eye rub of a tired child. this is deliberate, repetitive, hard pressing — with her knuckles, her fists, her fingertips, sometimes the heels of both hands pushing in simultaneously. she presses until her eyes are red and irritated. she presses until you can see the indentations in her skin. she presses with a focus and intensity that tells me she's getting something profound from this — something i don't understand but she clearly needs. she does it when she's bored and understimulated. she does it when she's excited and overstimulated. she does it in bright light and in complete darkness. she does it first thing in the morning and last thing at night. it's not connected to being tired, though she does it more when she's tired. it's not connected to eye discomfort — her eyes have been checked repeatedly. it's something else. something about the pressure, or the lights and patterns she sees when she presses, or both. our ophthalmologist is increasingly concerned. she's explained that chronic eye pressing can cause keratoconus — a thinning and bulging of the cornea. it can cause retinal damage. it can affect the shape of the eye and change her prescription dramatically. in children with already-compromised vision, it can accelerate vision loss. every time my daughter presses, she may be causing damage we can't see yet but will show up later. we've been told to stop her. but she can't stop. i've watched her try. we'll redirect her, and her hands will drift back to her eyes within seconds — sometimes while she's actively trying to do something else. it's not defiance. it's not attention-seeking. it's a compulsion, a need so deep and automatic that conscious intention can't override it. when we physically hold her hands away, she becomes frantic — not angry, but desperate, like we're depriving her of something essential. when we tried gloves, she screamed and bit at them. when we put arm splints on to prevent her elbows from bending, she found ways around them or became so distressed we couldn't continue. restraint isn't the answer. i've researched this endlessly. i know that eye pressing is common in children with visual impairments — it's called oculo-digital stimulation or the oculo-digital reflex. the pressing stimulates the retina mechanically, creating phosphenes — those lights and patterns you see when you press on closed eyes. for children with limited vision, this may be one of the few ways they can create visual experiences. but my daughter has functional vision. she can see well enough to navigate, to recognize people, to look at pictures. she's not blind. so why does she press? the other explanations i've found point to sensory seeking — specifically, proprioceptive input to the face, eye socket, and head. some children seem to crave deep pressure in these areas. the eye socket is one of the only places on the head where you can apply pressure directly to bone. some children need this input so intensely that they'll seek it even when it causes harm. some sources mention that eye pressing can be self-soothing, regulating, or a way to block out overwhelming visual input. some mention it as a stereotypy — a repetitive behavior that serves a sensory or regulatory function. some mention it alongside other self-injurious behaviors in children with developmental differences. what i understand is that my daughter's brain needs something that eye pressing provides. the solution isn't to simply stop the pressing — that just leaves her with an unmet need and the distress that comes with it. the solution is to find other ways to meet that need. safer ways. ways that give her the proprioceptive input, or the visual stimulation, or the regulatory effect — without the risk to her vision. i need materials that can replace eye pressing. things that give deep pressure to the face and head. things that create visual patterns and lights safely. things that keep her hands occupied. things that meet the need without the harm. because i can't watch her damage her eyes. but i also can't spend every moment in battle with her nervous system. there has to be another way?

acceptedAnswer.text: OCULO-DIGITAL STIMULATION / EYE PRESSING BEHAVIOR / VISUAL-PROPRIOCEPTIVE SELF-STIMULATION / STEREOTYPIC BEHAVIOR WITH POTENTIAL FOR SELF-INJURY! Eye pressing (also called eye poking, eye rubbing, or oculo-digital stimulation) is a repetitive behavior in which children apply pressure to their eyes using fingers, knuckles, fists, or other objects. This behavior is particularly common in children with visual impairments (where it may serve to generate visual stimulation through mechanical retinal activation, producing phosphenes — perceived light patterns), but also occurs in sighted children with autism, developmental delays, sensory processing differences, and other conditions. Eye pressing may serve multiple functions: proprioceptive input to the face and orbital area, visual self-stimulation through phosphene generation, self-soothing or regulation, blocking of overwhelming visual input, or meeting under-defined sensory needs. While the behavior meets a real sensory need, chronic eye pressing carries significant medical risks including keratoconus (corneal thinning and distortion), increased intraocular pressure, retinal detachment, enophthalmos (sunken eyes from fat atrophy), and progression of existing visual impairments. Intervention focuses on identifying the underlying sensory function and providing safer alternatives that meet the same need, rather than simple behavior extinction which leaves the need unmet.

mainEntity.name: What is Oculo-Digital Stimulation / Eye Pressing Behavior / Visual-Proprioceptive Self-Stimulation / Stereotypic Behavior with Potential for Self-Injury?

acceptedAnswer.text: Eye pressing (also called eye poking, eye rubbing, or oculo-digital stimulation) is a repetitive behavior in which children apply pressure to their eyes using fingers, knuckles, fists, or other objects. This behavior is particularly common in children with visual impairments (where it may serve to generate visual stimulation through mechanical retinal activation, producing phosphenes — perceived light patterns), but also occurs in sighted children with autism, developmental delays, sensory processing differences, and other conditions. Eye pressing may serve multiple functions: proprioceptive input to the face and orbital area, visual self-stimulation through phosphene generation, self-soothing or regulation, blocking of overwhelming visual input, or meeting under-defined sensory needs. While the behavior meets a real sensory need, chronic eye pressing carries significant medical risks including keratoconus (corneal thinning and distortion), increased intraocular pressure, retinal detachment, enophthalmos (sunken eyes from fat atrophy), and progression of existing visual impairments. Intervention focuses on identifying the underlying sensory function and providing safer alternatives that meet the same need, rather than simple behavior extinction which leaves the need unmet. Common signs include: Repetitive pressing, poking, or rubbing of eyes with fingers, knuckles, or fists, Pressing hard enough to cause redness, swelling, or indentations, Seeking out eye pressing during specific states (boredom, excitement, overwhelm, fatigue), Difficulty stopping despite redirection or consequences, Distress when physically prevented from pressing.

mainEntity.name: How does a Deep Pressure Face and Head Tools help with oculo-digital stimulation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Eye pressing often serves a proprioceptive function — providing deep pressure input to the face, eye socket area, and head. The eye socket is one of the few areas where pressure can reach bone directly, creating intense proprioceptive feedback. Deep pressure tools designed for the face and head can provide this input safely, without involving the eyes. Weighted eye masks (used on forehead and cheeks, not pressing on eyes), compression headbands, head wraps, and facial massage tools deliver proprioceptive input to the same general area. Firm pressure applied by caregivers to the forehead, temples, cheekbones, and jaw can also meet this need. The goal is to give the nervous system the deep pressure it craves in the orbital region without the actual eye contact that causes damage. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Use firm, consistent hand pressure on forehead, temples, cheekbones, and jaw. Create a weighted headband with rice or beads in fabric. Use a tight-fitting beanie or swim cap for compression. Apply deep pressure with a small, firm pillow to the face (avoiding eyes). Practice 'face squeezes' as a routine input activity. Safety: Never apply pressure directly to the eyes or eyeballs. Focus on surrounding areas: forehead, temples, cheekbones, jaw. Monitor for any discomfort or circulation issues with compression items.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Stimulation Alternatives help with oculo-digital stimulation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Eye pressing mechanically stimulates the retina, producing phosphenes — perceived patterns of light that can be quite complex and engaging. For children seeking visual stimulation, especially those with visual impairments, this may be one of the few ways they can generate intense visual experiences. Safe visual stimulation alternatives provide similar light patterns and visual interest without mechanical pressure. Fiber optic lamps, light spinners, kaleidoscopes, LED light toys, lava lamps, and projection devices create engaging visual patterns. Light-up toys that respond to touch or movement can provide the cause-and-effect visual feedback that eye pressing offers. The goal is to make external light sources more appealing than internally-generated phosphenes. Price: ₹400–2,500. DIY option: Use flashlight play in dark rooms. Create shadow patterns on walls. Use mirrors to reflect and multiply light sources. Make a simple light box with string lights. Use phone/tablet visual stimulation apps. Create a 'light corner' with multiple light sources. Safety: Avoid strobe effects or rapidly flashing lights that could trigger seizures in susceptible children. Monitor for eye strain from prolonged light viewing. Ensure light sources are not so bright they cause discomfort.

mainEntity.name: How does a Hand Occupation Fidgets and Tools help with oculo-digital stimulation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Eye pressing requires hands to be free and available. Keeping hands consistently occupied with engaging fidgets and tools reduces the opportunity for eye pressing while meeting tactile and proprioceptive needs through the hands instead. The most effective hand occupation tools provide similar sensory feedback to what the hands experience during pressing: deep pressure, resistance, interesting textures. Stress balls, therapy putty, fidget cubes, textured squeeze toys, resistance bands to pull, and items requiring two-handed engagement can redirect the seeking behavior. The goal isn't just to keep hands busy but to keep them busy with something sensorily satisfying enough to compete with eye pressing. Price: ₹100–800. DIY option: Make stress balls from balloons filled with flour or rice. Create resistance loops from thick rubber bands. Use large dried beans in a container for squeezing. Provide playdough or clay for constant manipulation. Offer thick rope or fabric for pulling and twisting. Safety: Ensure fidgets are large enough not to be choking hazards. Monitor for mouthing if the child mouths objects. Replace worn or damaged fidgets that could break into small pieces.

mainEntity.name: How does a Whole-Body Proprioceptive Input help with oculo-digital stimulation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Eye pressing is sometimes part of a broader need for proprioceptive input — and when the whole body is well-regulated with deep pressure and heavy work, the specific need to seek input through the eyes may decrease. Whole-body proprioceptive activities provide the nervous system with organizing input that can reduce the intensity of localized seeking behaviors. Weighted blankets, compression vests, body socks, heavy work activities (carrying, pushing, pulling), joint compressions, and deep pressure techniques flood the system with the input it craves. When proprioceptive needs are met globally, the urgency of meeting them through eye pressing may diminish. Price: ₹500–3,000. DIY option: Create a 'burrito roll' with heavy blankets for deep pressure. Have child push heavy laundry baskets or furniture. Use cushion squishes with child between pillows. Do wheelbarrow walking, bear crawls, or wall push-ups. Apply joint compressions to arms, legs, and shoulders. Fill a backpack with books for wearing. Safety: Follow weight guidelines for weighted items (typically not exceeding 10% of body weight). Supervise body sock use to prevent entanglement. Ensure crash pad activities are supervised.

mainEntity.name: How does a Orbital-Area Massage and Pressure Protocols help with oculo-digital stimulation?

acceptedAnswer.text: Structured massage and pressure protocols for the face — particularly the areas around the eyes without touching the eyes themselves — can be provided as scheduled sensory input, reducing the need for the child to seek this input independently through pressing. Occupational therapists can teach specific pressure routines for the brow bone, temples, cheekbones, bridge of the nose, and jaw that provide intense proprioceptive input. When delivered on a schedule (sensory diet approach), this input can be 'preventive' — meeting the need before the child seeks it through harmful behavior. Parent-delivered facial pressure routines, professional facial massage, or structured self-massage protocols can all serve this function. Price: ₹0–500 (mostly technique-based). DIY option: Learn a consistent pressure routine: firm pressure to brow bone (5 seconds), temples (5 seconds), cheekbones (5 seconds), bridge of nose (5 seconds), jaw (5 seconds). Deliver this sequence on schedule — morning, after meals, before challenging times. Use consistent pressure and rhythm. Teach child to request 'face squeezes' as an alternative. Safety: Never apply pressure directly to the eyes. Focus on bone areas around the orbits. Use firm but not painful pressure. Watch for signs of discomfort. Consult with OT for proper technique.

mainEntity.name: How does a Blocking and Protective Gear help with oculo-digital stimulation?

acceptedAnswer.text: While replacement strategies are the primary intervention, protective gear may be needed to prevent damage while those strategies are being developed and generalized. Protective glasses with impact-resistant lenses, soft eye shields, arm splints that prevent elbow bending (so hands can't reach face), and modified mittens can create barriers. However, these work best as part of a comprehensive plan — buying time while replacement behaviors are taught — rather than as standalone solutions. Blocking without replacement often increases distress and can intensify the behavior when protection is removed. Protective gear should be comfortable enough for extended wear and paired with active teaching of alternatives. Price: ₹300–2,000. DIY option: Start with wrap-around glasses or swimming goggles for visual barrier. Use long sleeves with thumbholes to reduce knuckle access to eyes. Create visual reminders on hands (stickers, drawings) that cue redirection. Safety: Protective gear must be comfortable for extended wear. Never use restraining devices unsupervised or as punishment. Monitor for distress and have a plan for how long protective gear will be needed. Pair protection with active replacement teaching.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Oculo Digital Stimulation
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Eye pressing is a sensory behavior that serves a function, not a behavioral problem to be eliminated through willpower or punishment. The function — whether proprioceptive input to the orbital area, visual stimulation through phosphene generation, self-regulation, or some combination — must be identified and met through safer alternatives. Blocking without replacement creates distress and leaves the underlying need unmet, often intensifying the behavior when blocking is removed. Functional replacement is the sustainable path: identify what the pressing provides, and provide it through materials and strategies that don't risk vision.

step.text: Eye pressing (also called eye poking, eye rubbing, or oculo-digital stimulation) is a repetitive behavior in which children apply pressure to their eyes using fingers, knuckles, fists, or other objects. This behavior is particularly common in children with visual impairments (where it may serve to generate visual stimulation through mechanical retinal activation, producing phosphenes — perceived light patterns), but also occurs in sighted children with autism, developmental delays, sensory processing differences, and other conditions. Eye pressing may serve multiple functions: proprioceptive input to the face and orbital area, visual self-stimulation through phosphene generation, self-soothing or regulation, blocking of overwhelming visual input, or meeting under-defined sensory needs. While the behavior meets a real sensory need, chronic eye pressing carries significant medical risks including keratoconus (corneal thinning and distortion), increased intraocular pressure, retinal detachment, enophthalmos (sunken eyes from fat atrophy), and progression of existing visual impairments. Intervention focuses on identifying the underlying sensory function and providing safer alternatives that meet the same need, rather than simple behavior extinction which leaves the need unmet. signOrSymptom.name: Repetitive pressing, poking, or rubbing of eyes with fingers, knuckles, or fists
Pressing hard enough to cause redness, swelling, or indentations
Seeking out eye pressing during specific states (boredom, excitement, overwhelm, fatigue)
Difficulty stopping despite redirection or consequences
Distress when physically prevented from pressing
Visible enjoyment or calm during pressing episodes
Pressing in darkness (suggesting light generation is part of function)
Dark circles or discoloration around eyes from chronic pressure

citation.name: Research on Oculo-Digital Stimulation and Sensory Replacement
citation.description: Research on eye pressing (oculo-digital stimulation) documents its prevalence in children with visual impairment and developmental disabilities, its mechanisms (phosphene generation, proprioceptive seeking), its risks (keratoconus, retinal damage, orbital changes), and intervention approaches focused on functional replacement rather than simple extinction. Studies emphasize that the behavior serves sensory functions that must be addressed for sustainable reduction.

citation.name: Ophthalmological Literature on Eye Pressing Complications
citation.description: Medical literature documents keratoconus, increased intraocular pressure, retinal changes, and enophthalmos associated with chronic eye pressing, emphasizing need for intervention and monitoring

citation.name: Occupational Therapy Sensory Integration Research
citation.description: OT literature on sensory integration and sensory diets supports scheduled proprioceptive input and environmental modification as strategies for reducing harmful sensory-seeking behaviors

item.name: Visual Stimulation Alternatives
item.description: Eye pressing mechanically stimulates the retina, producing phosphenes — perceived patterns of light that can be quite complex and engaging. For children seeking visual stimulation, especially those with visual impairments, this may be one of the few ways they can generate intense visual experiences. Safe visual stimulation alternatives provide similar light patterns and visual interest without mechanical pressure. Fiber optic lamps, light spinners, kaleidoscopes, LED light toys, lava lamps, and projection devices create engaging visual patterns. Light-up toys that respond to touch or movement can provide the cause-and-effect visual feedback that eye pressing offers. The goal is to make external light sources more appealing than internally-generated phosphenes.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use flashlight play in dark rooms. Create shadow patterns on walls. Use mirrors to reflect and multiply light sources. Make a simple light box with string lights. Use phone/tablet visual stimulation apps. Create a 'light corner' with multiple light sources.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Avoid strobe effects or rapidly flashing lights that could trigger seizures in susceptible children. Monitor for eye strain from prolonged light viewing. Ensure light sources are not so bright they cause discomfort.

item.name: Hand Occupation Fidgets and Tools
item.description: Eye pressing requires hands to be free and available. Keeping hands consistently occupied with engaging fidgets and tools reduces the opportunity for eye pressing while meeting tactile and proprioceptive needs through the hands instead. The most effective hand occupation tools provide similar sensory feedback to what the hands experience during pressing: deep pressure, resistance, interesting textures. Stress balls, therapy putty, fidget cubes, textured squeeze toys, resistance bands to pull, and items requiring two-handed engagement can redirect the seeking behavior. The goal isn't just to keep hands busy but to keep them busy with something sensorily satisfying enough to compete with eye pressing.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Make stress balls from balloons filled with flour or rice. Create resistance loops from thick rubber bands. Use large dried beans in a container for squeezing. Provide playdough or clay for constant manipulation. Offer thick rope or fabric for pulling and twisting.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure fidgets are large enough not to be choking hazards. Monitor for mouthing if the child mouths objects. Replace worn or damaged fidgets that could break into small pieces.

item.name: Whole-Body Proprioceptive Input
item.description: Eye pressing is sometimes part of a broader need for proprioceptive input — and when the whole body is well-regulated with deep pressure and heavy work, the specific need to seek input through the eyes may decrease. Whole-body proprioceptive activities provide the nervous system with organizing input that can reduce the intensity of localized seeking behaviors. Weighted blankets, compression vests, body socks, heavy work activities (carrying, pushing, pulling), joint compressions, and deep pressure techniques flood the system with the input it craves. When proprioceptive needs are met globally, the urgency of meeting them through eye pressing may diminish.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a 'burrito roll' with heavy blankets for deep pressure. Have child push heavy laundry baskets or furniture. Use cushion squishes with child between pillows. Do wheelbarrow walking, bear crawls, or wall push-ups. Apply joint compressions to arms, legs, and shoulders. Fill a backpack with books for wearing.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Follow weight guidelines for weighted items (typically not exceeding 10% of body weight). Supervise body sock use to prevent entanglement. Ensure crash pad activities are supervised.

item.name: Orbital-Area Massage and Pressure Protocols
item.description: Structured massage and pressure protocols for the face — particularly the areas around the eyes without touching the eyes themselves — can be provided as scheduled sensory input, reducing the need for the child to seek this input independently through pressing. Occupational therapists can teach specific pressure routines for the brow bone, temples, cheekbones, bridge of the nose, and jaw that provide intense proprioceptive input. When delivered on a schedule (sensory diet approach), this input can be 'preventive' — meeting the need before the child seeks it through harmful behavior. Parent-delivered facial pressure routines, professional facial massage, or structured self-massage protocols can all serve this function.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Learn a consistent pressure routine: firm pressure to brow bone (5 seconds), temples (5 seconds), cheekbones (5 seconds), bridge of nose (5 seconds), jaw (5 seconds). Deliver this sequence on schedule — morning, after meals, before challenging times. Use consistent pressure and rhythm. Teach child to request 'face squeezes' as an alternative.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never apply pressure directly to the eyes. Focus on bone areas around the orbits. Use firm but not painful pressure. Watch for signs of discomfort. Consult with OT for proper technique.

item.name: Blocking and Protective Gear
item.description: While replacement strategies are the primary intervention, protective gear may be needed to prevent damage while those strategies are being developed and generalized. Protective glasses with impact-resistant lenses, soft eye shields, arm splints that prevent elbow bending (so hands can't reach face), and modified mittens can create barriers. However, these work best as part of a comprehensive plan — buying time while replacement behaviors are taught — rather than as standalone solutions. Blocking without replacement often increases distress and can intensify the behavior when protection is removed. Protective gear should be comfortable enough for extended wear and paired with active teaching of alternatives.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Start with wrap-around glasses or swimming goggles for visual barrier. Use long sleeves with thumbholes to reduce knuckle access to eyes. Create visual reminders on hands (stickers, drawings) that cue redirection.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Protective gear must be comfortable for extended wear. Never use restraining devices unsupervised or as punishment. Monitor for distress and have a plan for how long protective gear will be needed. Pair protection with active replacement teaching.

item.name: Sensory Rooms and Visual Regulation Spaces
item.description: Dedicated sensory spaces that provide rich visual and proprioceptive input can meet the needs that drive eye pressing in an immersive, safe environment. A sensory room or corner with fiber optics, bubble tubes, projections, and opportunities for deep pressure gives the child a place where their sensory needs are abundantly met — reducing the urgency of meeting them through harmful self-stimulation. When the child is in a sensory space, they're receiving the input through the environment rather than having to create it through their body. Regular access to well-designed sensory spaces can be a key component of a sensory diet that reduces eye pressing throughout the day.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a sensory corner with string lights, fabric hangings, and a bean bag chair. Add a fiber optic spray (affordable options available). Include weighted blankets and compression options. Make it a 'go-to' space when input is needed. Use dark tent or canopy to enhance light effects.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure sensory space is safe for unsupervised time if that's the intention. Secure electrical cords and avoid tip-hazards. Monitor for overstimulation as well as understimulation.

item.name: Regulation and Alertness Tools
item.description: Eye pressing often correlates with arousal states — occurring during under-arousal (boredom, low energy) or over-arousal (excitement, overwhelm). Tools that help maintain optimal alertness and regulation can reduce the frequency of eye pressing by keeping the child in a regulated state where the behavior is less likely to occur. Alerting inputs (movement, crunchy foods, cold water, vibration) can address under-arousal. Calming inputs (deep pressure, slow rhythmic movement, heavy work) can address over-arousal. Understanding which arousal state triggers the child's eye pressing guides which regulation tools to emphasize. When the child is well-regulated, the need to self-regulate through eye pressing decreases.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Identify which arousal state triggers pressing (bored/under-aroused vs. overwhelmed/over-aroused). For under-arousal: movement breaks, crunchy snacks, cold water, bright environment. For over-arousal: deep pressure, slow movement, dim lighting, reduced stimulation. Build regulation strategies into daily schedule.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Observe patterns to understand the child's specific triggers. What works for under-arousal may worsen over-arousal and vice versa. Individualize the approach.

item.name: Behavioral Replacement Training
item.description: Beyond providing materials, structured behavioral training teaches the child to use alternatives actively and automatically. This involves identifying the function of eye pressing, selecting appropriate replacements, teaching those replacements during calm times, prompting replacement when pressing urges arise, and reinforcing successful use of alternatives. Visual supports (choice boards showing alternative behaviors), social stories about protecting eyes and using alternatives, and consistent prompting protocols help the child learn new patterns. The goal is for alternative behaviors to become as automatic as eye pressing currently is. This requires practice, consistency across all environments, and patience — replacing an established behavior takes time.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a visual choice board with 3-4 alternatives to eye pressing. Practice alternatives during calm times, not just during pressing urges. Prompt immediately when hands move toward face ('hands here instead'). Reinforce every use of alternatives. Use social story about protecting eyes. Be consistent across all environments and caregivers.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Replacement training takes time. Expect the process to take weeks to months, not days. Avoid punishment for eye pressing — focus on reinforcing alternatives. Seek behavioral therapy support for intensive patterns.

socialMedia.caption: When pressing on their eyes gives them something they desperately need — you can't just stop it, you have to replace it. Eye pressing meets deep sensory needs, but it can damage vision. Here are 9 materials that provide the same input safely: deep pressure face tools, visual stimulation alternatives, hand occupation fidgets, whole-body proprioceptive input, facial pressure routines, protective gear, sensory rooms, regulation tools, and behavioral replacement training. Meet the need, protect the eyes. Save to build your replacement plan! 🔖 #EyePressing #SensoryReplacement #PinnacleBlooms
